title: The welcome posthumous pardon for Ruth Ellis
gist: A lawyer argues that England's posthumous pardons (Ruth Ellis, Turing, WWI deserters) are the state forgiving itself for cruel laws rather than court errors, and that mercy should extend to the living, not just the historical dead.

insights.claim: At law a pardon is about mercy, not justice: the conviction stands and serves justice, while the pardon merely relieves the offender of the sanctions that would otherwise be imposed.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Clarifies that a pardon is not an exoneration—relevant for how legal-reform and rehabilitation debates are framed.

insights.claim: Pardons are rare in England but there is 'a keen trade in them' in the United States, and they were widely used in Northern Ireland during the Troubles though records are not public.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Signals sharply different pardon cultures across common-law jurisdictions.

insights.claim: Pardons are part of the royal prerogative but, unlike most prerogative powers, are generally outside judicial review; courts held in the 1993 Derek Bentley case they have 'no power to direct the way in which the prerogative of mercy should be exercised'.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Marks a rare zone of near-unreviewable executive discretion in UK constitutional law.
insights.locator: Pardons are part of the royal prerogative

insights.claim: Posthumous pardons for Derek Bentley, WWI soldiers executed for desertion, Alan Turing and Ruth Ellis were granted for mistakes in the laws themselves being cruel and unjust, not for courts making errors of law or evidence.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Frames pardons as an admission that historical statutes—not judicial process—were the failure.
insights.locator: And there have subsequently been several

insights.claim: Posthumous pardons have no practical effect and are symbolic acts in which the state is trying to forgive itself for its own legislative mistakes—'the state begging its own pardon'.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A skeptical read on symbolic state gestures that reframes them as self-exculpation.
insights.quote: They are symbolic: it is the state begging its own pardon.
insights.locator: And so the “forgiveness” the state is trying to offer

insights.claim: The courts in 1993 held the prerogative of mercy is 'now a constitutional safeguard against mistakes', legitimising posthumous conditional pardons where a death sentence has already been carried out.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Establishes the doctrinal basis on which future posthumous pardons rest.

insights.claim: The state prefers granting posthumous pardons because it is reluctant to admit mistakes when affected people are still alive; the Steve Gallant case, where a prisoner saved lives in a terror attack, was quickly framed as controversial.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Explains a structural bias toward symbolic, low-risk pardons over consequential living ones.
insights.locator: What the state should also be doing

insights.claim: Pardons should not be confined to historical cases and should be actively considered for the living who can actually benefit from the mercy.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A policy prescription to watch if constitutional-reform momentum builds under a new government.
insights.quote: Pardons should not be just for cases from the history books.
insights.locator: But the ultimate question is whether mercy
